food_name: Dodol (Palm Sugar and Coconut Milk Sticky Candy)
region_of_origin: Malay Archipelago — Malaysia, Singapore, Indonesia (Betawi and Sundanese origin; Muar, Johor variant most prominent in Singapore)
food_subgroup: Traditional palm sugar and coconut milk confection; dense, sticky, dark-brown candy made from gula melaka, thick coconut milk (santan), and glutinous rice flour, slow-cooked for 4–8 hours

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Dodol. No exact URI available. Closest parent concept is a palm sugar coconut confection or glutinous rice-based sweet. Null is appropriate pending FoodOn expansion.
ontology.foodex2_description: Sugar confectionery (closest parent — FoodEx2 level 3 sugar confectionery category; no specific Dodol code exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No specific FoodEx2 code for Dodol. A039K (sugar confectionery) used as closest defensible parent. Exact match not available.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept for Dodol or equivalent traditional Malay confection as of 2024.

allergens: glutinous rice (gluten-free grain but cross-contamination risk possible)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of dodol is 2 individually wrapped pieces weighing approximately 40g (each piece ~20g); at Hari Raya gatherings, 4–6 pieces (80–120g) may be consumed casually.

nutrient_source.title: Dodol — IMR Malaysia MyFCD (Malaysian Food Composition Database)
nutrient_source.organization: Institute for Medical Research Malaysia — Malaysian Food Composition Database (MyFCD), Ministry of Health Malaysia
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the IMR Malaysia MyFCD entry for Dodol. IMR Malaysia MyFCD is an approved primary regional source under Project Veritas v2 source policy for Malaysian dishes. Values are accepted as verified with confidence 1.0 per input record provenance.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Palm Sugar (Gula Melaka) and Glutinous Rice-Based Confections — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for palm sugar and glutinous rice products
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (glycemicindex.com) — analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Dodol exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI ~68 is estimated from: (1) palm sugar (gula melaka) GI approximately 35–54 per University of Sydney GI Database entries for palm sugar; (2) glutinous rice GI approximately 86–98 per University of Sydney GI Database; (3) the combined effect of slow-cooking, high sugar density, and fat content from coconut milk moderating the overall glycemic response. The blended estimate of GI ~68 (medium-high boundary) is consistent with the food's macronutrient profile. GL per 40g serving = (68 × 23.2g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 15.8 (HIGH for a single 40g serving). Confidence 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (30mg/100g; 12mg per 40g serving); secondary concern is high saturated fat from coconut milk (11.5g/100g) with cardiovascular implications
hypertension.guidance: Dodol poses minimal sodium risk for hypertension patients (12mg per 40g serving), but the very high saturated fat content (4.6g per 40g serving) from coconut milk warrants limiting intake to 1–2 pieces occasionally to avoid cumulative cardiovascular fat load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Extremely high sugar density (52g sugar/100g, predominantly from palm sugar), high total carbohydrate (58g/100g), GI ~68, and glycemic load of 15.8 per 40g serving (2 pieces)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Dodol is one of the highest-glycemic-concern traditional festival foods for T2DM patients — a 40g serving (2 pieces) delivers 23.2g sugar and a GL of ~15.8; patients should limit to 1 piece (20g) maximum per occasion and monitor post-prandial glucose, as casual consumption of 4–6 pieces (80–120g) delivers a GL of 32–47.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat from coconut milk (11.5g/100g = 4.6g per 40g serving) contributing to LDL-raising saturated fatty acid load; zero trans fat and very low sodium are mitigating factors
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high saturated fat content (predominantly from coconut milk lauric acid) raises LDL cholesterol risk with regular consumption; CVD patients should limit dodol to 1 piece (20g) per occasion and avoid frequent intake during festive seasons when multiple high-saturated-fat foods are consumed together.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Very high sugar content promotes AGE (advanced glycation end-product) formation which accumulates in inflamed synovial tissue; high saturated fat adds pro-inflammatory lipid load
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: RA patients with active disease should limit dodol to 1 piece (20g) maximum per occasion, as the combination of 52g sugar/100g and 11.5g saturated fat/100g promotes both AGE formation and pro-inflammatory lipid signalling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dodol is one of the most labour-intensive and culturally significant traditional confections of the Malay Archipelago — a dense, shiny, dark mahogany-brown candy made by slow-cooking three core ingredients: gula melaka (palm sugar), thick coconut milk (santan), and glutinous rice flour, together over a wood fire or gas flame for 4–8 hours of continuous stirring until the mixture becomes so thick it pulls away from the pot in a single cohesive mass. The result is extraordinarily dense, sticky, and intensely sweet, with a complex caramel-coconut-molasses flavour profile derived from the palm sugar's natural mineral richness. Dodol is sold in individually wrapped pieces or sliced from a block, and is a quintessential Hari Raya Aidilfitri sweetmeat in Singapore and Malaysia. The Muar (Johor, Malaysia) variety is the most commercially prominent and widely exported to Singapore. Nutritionally, dodol is one of the most glycemically challenging traditional festival foods in Singapore: 52g sugar per 100g, GI approximately 68 (medium-high boundary), and very high saturated fat (11.5g/100g) from coconut milk. The common belief that palm sugar is 'healthier' has a factual basis — gula melaka GI is approximately 35–54, lower than white sugar (~65) — but at 52g total sugar per 100g, the absolute sugar load overwhelms any glycemic index advantage. For T2DM patients, 1 piece (20g) is the absolute maximum culturally-sensitive recommendation per occasion. Energy: 370 kcal/100g; protein: 2g; fat: 14g (saturated fat 11.5g); carbohydrate: 58g; sugar: 52g; sodium: 30mg; dietary fibre: 0.5g.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the IMR Malaysia MyFCD (Institute for Medical Research Malaysia — Malaysian Food Composition Database), which is an approved primary regional source for Malaysian dishes under Project Veritas v2. All core macros and secondary nutrients are present with confidence 1.0 per input provenance. GI is an analogous estimate (confidence 0.9) based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for palm sugar and glutinous rice, which is the approved GI source.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for dodol not available in IMR Malaysia MyFCD or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content for dodol not reported in IMR Malaysia MyFCD as of 2026. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per IMR Malaysia MyFCD; dodol contains no hydrogenated fats by traditional formulation. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero; dodol is a plant-based confection with no animal-derived cholesterol sources. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_b12_mcg are 0.0 — confirmed negligible/zero per IMR Malaysia MyFCD; dodol ingredients (palm sugar, coconut milk, glutinous rice flour) do not contribute meaningful amounts of these vitamins. GI of ~68 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database entries for palm sugar (GI 35–54) and glutinous rice (GI 86–98); no direct GI measurement for dodol exists in the University of Sydney database. GL per 40g serving calculated as (68 × 23.2g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 15.8. Two default portions exist in source data (40g and 50g); 40g (2 pieces × ~20g each) used as primary serving size per food name. FoodEx2 code A039K (sugar confectionery) used as closest defensible parent — no specific Dodol code exists in FoodEx2. Dodol is a Malay/Indonesian dish; multilingual names provided for Malay, Chinese (simplified and traditional), Tamil, Hindi, and Indonesian per Project Veritas v2 multilingual rules for Singaporean/Malay cuisine context.
